name: Dyslipidemia
body:
**Dyslipidemia** is a quantitative or qualitative alteration of plasma lipid metabolism. In clinical practice it is classified according to the predominant profile: **hypercholesterolemia** (elevated total cholesterol and/or LDL-C), **hypertriglyceridemia** (elevated triglycerides), or **mixed dyslipidemia**. Its relevance lies in increased **atherosclerotic cardiovascular risk** and, in severe hypertriglyceridemia, the risk of **acute pancreatitis**.

Evaluation requires a fasting lipid profile (or interpretation according to current guidelines), family history, comorbidities (diabetes, hypothyroidism, nephrotic syndrome, obesity, alcohol use), and estimation of global risk (smoking, hypertension, age, sex). **LDL-C** is the main therapeutic target in primary and secondary prevention; target values depend on risk stratum. In the emergency department or inpatient setting, dyslipidemia usually presents as uncontrolled comorbidity, drug interaction (corticosteroids, immunosuppressants, antiretrovirals), or, exceptionally, as **severe hypertriglyceridemia** (> 500–1000 mg/dL) with epigastric pain suggestive of pancreatitis.

**Treatment** combines lifestyle modifications (Mediterranean or low saturated-fat diet, physical activity, smoking cessation) and pharmacotherapy per guideline: high-intensity statins in secondary cardiovascular prevention; fibrates or omega-3 in severe hypertriglyceridemia; and control of secondary causes. In the hospital setting, prioritize reinstating or continuing lipid-lowering therapy when not contraindicated, avoid discontinuing statins in acute coronary syndrome unless expressly indicated, and coordinate with nutrition and the treating medical team.

**Nursing:** record lipid profile and blood glucose on admission when clinically appropriate; identify prior adherence to statins or fibrates; monitor hepatic enzymes and CK if treatment is initiated or intensified; educate on diet, exercise, and signs of severe myalgia or jaundice; in severe hypertriglyceridemia, monitor abdominal pain, nausea, and amylase/lipase per protocol.

bibliography.citation: European Society of Cardiology / European Atherosclerosis Society. Guidelines for the management of dyslipidaemias.
Sociedad Argentina de Cardiología (SAC). Cardiovascular prevention and risk factor management.
Ministerio de Salud de la Nación Argentina. Care strategies and guidelines.
American Heart Association / American College of Cardiology. Guideline on the management of blood cholesterol.
